qas.q: When you think of Resource conservation and recovery act (RCRA) THINK...
qas.a: hazardous waste
Occupational Health and Safety Act.. covered under 29 CFR 1910.120
Competence for responders to Hazardous Material
Competence for EMS personnel

qas.q: What is an Awareness Level Responder
qas.a: They are expected to recognize, protect themselves, call for trained personnel, secure the area, evacuate, and deny entry

qas.q: While analyzing an incident the goal is to...
qas.a: Detect, survey, collect, and implement actions consistent with AHJ (Authority having jurisdiction)
set perimeters and zones using the ERG. Deny entry to the contaminated area and assist with evacuate
initiate the incident command system, ensuring the protection of responders and the public
A substance (either matter-solid, liquid, or gas- or energy) that when released causes harm to PEOPLE, ENVIRONMENT, and, PROPERTY
to cover the 9 hazard classes
that if released into the environment above a certain amount, it must be reported
for chemicals that must be reported if released above a THRESHOLD
chemicals whose total emissions or release be reported Annually by owners that manufacture, process, or otherwise use a listed toxic chemicals
to denote any chemical that would be a risk to employees in the workplace
In united nations model codes and regulations, hazardous materials are known as
chemicals that possess toxic, reactive, flammable, or explosive property
Any weapon involving toxic or poisonous chemicals, disease organism, or designed to release radiation

Class 1 : Explosives
Major hazard: Explosion
Mass explosion hazard ex: Dynamite, T-N-T
Projection Hazard ex: Detonation Cord
Fire Hazard ex: Liquid- fueled rocket motors
Minor explosion hazard ex: ammunition
very insensitive explosives ex: Prilled ammunition nitrate fertilizer
extremely insensitive articles ex: Firecracker

Class 2: gases

major hazards: BLEVE (Boiling liquid expanding vapor explosion

Sub-hazards: Flammable, oxidizer, and poisonous
Flammable gas ex: Propane

Non-flammable, non- poisonous ex: compressed

OXYGEN

A cryogenic liquid
Poisonous Gas ex: Chlorine

Class 3: Flammable and Combustible Liquids

Major Hazard: Burns readily

Flash point of not more than 140 degrees F
ex: acetone, amyl acetate, methyl alcohol, toluene, gasoline

Flash point above 140 degrees F and below 200 degrees F
ex: mineral oil, fuel oil, peanut oil

Class 4: Flammable and Combustible

Major hazard: Rapid Combustion with a liberation of mass quantities of smoke

Flammable Solid
DENSITIZED EXPLOSIVES( to suppress explosion)
SELF- REACTIVE MATERIALS
READILY COMBUSTIBLE SOLIDS
ex: magnesium (pellets, turnings, and ribbons), Nitrocellulose

Spontaneously combustible material
PYROPHORIC MATERIAL
SELF-HEATING MATERIAL
ex: phorphorus
A liquid or solid that , even in small quantities and without an external ignition source, can ignite within five minutes after coming in contact with air

Dangerous when wet
"MRE heaters"
calcium carbide
magnesium powder

Supports combustion, intensifies fire

ex: ammonium nitrate

Unstable/ reactive explosives
ex: Methyl ethyl ketone peroxide
Oxidizers

Type A: forbidden for transport
Type B: Can undergo thermal explosion
Type C: Cant undergo thermal explo.
Type D. medium to no effect when heated under confinement
Type E: low to no effect when heated
Type F: low or no explosive powe
Type G: Desensitized

Poison
major hazard: toxicity, infectious
Poisonous material, other than a gas can cause irritation. "CS"

Infectious substance, contain pathogens and cause disease in humans or animals

ex: anthrax, botulism

Radioactive
Major Hazard: Radioactive poisonous burns
ex: uranium 0.002 mircocuries/gram
yellowcake

Corrosive
Major Hazard: Burns/Emulsification skin damage
ex: sulfuric acid and nitric acid

Miscellaneous
A material that presents a hazard during transport, but is not included in another hazard class
Labels for materials that present a limited hazard during transport due to its form, quantity and packaging ex: consumer commodities " furniture polish/ fingernail polish"

qas.q: Incidents Involving WMDs may have additional differences including
qas.a:
INTENT- inflict harm, and kill
SEVERITY AND COMPLEXITY - large number of causalities
CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT-preservation of evidence
INCIDENT COMMAND-law enforcement will have jurisdiction
SECONDARY DEVICES- booby traps, use of weapons, armed resistance and assault, secondary events

qas.q: Locations where WMD are manufactured transported, stored, used, or disposed of are:
qas.a:
warehouses
tank farms
weapon depots
hospitals
labs
truck terminals
flight line areas
maintenance facilities

Type A: protective overpacks
Type B: Casks
